**Refining Website Concepts**

I'm currently focused on the 'Anvitam' website. The client loves 'blurringboundaries.in' and wants a similar vibe. I'm nailing down page requirements: a home landing page and a projects grid. This website needs a great UI/UX.


**Developing Functionality Further**

I've expanded the website's scope significantly. Beyond the initial homepage and project grid, I'm now thinking about specific sections: 'Team,' 'Awards & Press,' 'Contact' with relevant Mumbai info, and a blog. The admin panel is taking shape, allowing for dynamic project and blog content updates. Design-wise, I'm sticking to minimalism with architectural influences - whitespace, large visuals, and clean typography.


**Finalizing Technical Specifications**

I've solidified the technical architecture. React 18+, TypeScript, ESM, and Tailwind are confirmed. I'll use a single XML output with metadata.json and handle responsiveness. A sticky CTA is integrated, and `createRoot` is implemented. I'm avoiding `react-dropzone` and keeping `picsum.photos` for images.  I'll include a small analytics dashboard using `d3` or `recharts` in the admin section.  Integrating Gemini API for content generation within the admin panel seems like a great fit and fulfills the "Senior Engineer" prompt.


**Constructing the App**

I've sketched out the React component structure, defining key files like `App.tsx` for routing and layout, `types.ts` for data interfaces, and `constants.ts` with mock data. I'm focusing on a minimalist approach, evident in the `Navbar` and `Footer` components. The core of the website's functionality is going into place, with components for the project cards, admin dashboard, and content editor, which will integrate the Gemini API for project and blog content generation.


**Adding Content Dynamically**

I'm now implementing the admin panel's core feature: dynamic content management. I'll utilize React Context or local state to simulate a "database" of projects and blog posts, given the absence of a backend. Users will be able to add new items.  A "Magic Generate" button is planned within the admin form, leveraging the Gemini API to craft detailed architectural descriptions from user-provided keywords.

**Refining UI Aesthetics**

The UI style is coming together. I'm focusing on a clean, sans-serif font (Inter or Tailwind's default) against a stark white/black/gray palette. Asymmetrical grids, expansive whitespace, and full-width images will be used to enhance the visual appeal.


**Developing Admin Panel Functionality**

I've made progress on the admin panel. I'm focusing on the admin interface with mock data for projects and blog posts, using `constants.ts` and dynamic additions through the admin panel. The "Magic Generate" button is now integrated, leveraging the Gemini API to produce architectural descriptions based on user keywords. I'm also implementing the necessary infrastructure, including an `Admin` link in the footer with a mock login.


**Building Site Components**

I'm now focusing on specific components for the website. I have `ContentContext.tsx` for state management, the main layout elements (Navbar, Footer), the critical `ProjectCard`, an admin data display chart (`AdminChart`), and the individual page structures for Home, Projects, Team, Blog, Contact, and Admin. The overall application structure is being handled by `App.tsx`.


**Coding the Admin Interface**

I'm now starting on the admin interface. I'm focusing on the Admin Chart (using Recharts) for a basic site analytics overview. I'm also readying for Gemini API integration within the content editor to generate project descriptions. I'll stick to a simple file input instead of react-dropzone. I'm being mindful of the useEffect dependency pitfalls to avoid infinite loops. The core app structure is coming together with content management on the Admin page.


**Structuring File Hierarchy**

I've organized the file structure, aiming for manageable component counts by combining smaller components. Key files include `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, and crucial data, context, and service files. I'm focusing on well-defined layout, page components, and a dedicated `geminiService.ts` for AI integration. I'll stick to a standard CDN for Tailwind. I need to make sure XML is valid and no text is outside the CDATA.

we are

BLURRING BOUNDARIES

blurring boundaries
Architects in Mumbai, India Blending Sustainability with Nature
scroll

what
Our work is a tapestry of ideas shaped into reality. Each project is a story—born from collaboration, rooted in context, and guided by sustainability and nature. Together with our clients and partners, we bring visions to life, creating spaces that inspire and endure.

eleve
Goa
Tucked into the lush, heritage-rich village of Assagao—often called Goa’s “Village of Flowers” —this upcoming resort by Blurring Boundaries is envisioned as a tranquil retreat that honors both nature and culture. Drawing from the village’s Indo-Portuguese charm and its vibrant creative community, the project seeks to create a space where architecture, ecology, and local identity coexist in harmony.

View project
Eleve 1
Asmalay
Alibag
Located within a lush tapestry of mango trees, ‘Asmalay‘ is a biophilic farmhouse in Alibag, near Mumbai that embraces the harmonious interplay of architecture and nature. Through a thoughtful fusion of form and function, the design seeks to create immersive experiences where spatial arrangement, materials, lighting, and colors synergize to establish a sense of harmony. 

View project
Asmalay 1
Maativan
Wada
Maativan Wada, signifies a natural building sanctuary made of mud, amidst the forest’s embrace. It is nestled on the fringes of Wada’s reserved forest area near Mumbai in India, stands as a testament to dedication to environmental consciousness through the integration of material palette, passive design climatology principles and construction technology.

View project
09 maativan blurring boundaries1
Lake house
Surat
The beauty of the structure is in the blend of materials and the play of light and shadow of the same. Designed by architects in Surat, the structure has landscape elements embedded both within and without, playing a very important role in enhancing the design. The green elements seen throughout the structure liven up the spaces and work to bring nature into the life of the user.

View project
Lake house
view all projects
why
Design is our way of connecting people, place, and nature. Guided by principles of sustainability, contextual sensitivity, and biophilia, we see architecture as more than buildings—it is about creating harmony, telling stories, and nurturing life.

Sustainable
We design with responsibility toward the environment, minimizing resource use while maximizing efficiency and longevity. Passive strategies ensure natural light, ventilation, and comfortable environments, while active technologies like solar energy, rainwater harvesting, and waste management strengthen performance. Economic sustainability is equally central — we embrace local, natural materials and eco-friendly construction methods such as rat-trap bond walls, filler slabs, arches, and mud construction. In doing so, we create buildings that respect both people and planet.

Contextual
Our designs evolve from the lives and needs of the people who inhabit them. We respond to client requirements and lifestyles while shaping spaces that evoke the right emotions and experiences. Every project is rooted in its surroundings—climate, culture, and socio-economic context—so that materials, forms, and spatial arrangements feel authentic to their place. By balancing personal aspirations with environmental and cultural sensitivity, we create architecture that belongs to both people and setting.

Contextual architects in india
Biophilic
We design to nurture the innate human connection with nature. Natural light, ventilation, greenery, and water are woven into spaces to support health, well-being, and productivity. Organic forms, textures, and materials create a sensory richness that brings balance and calm, while preserving existing trees and ecosystems strengthens biodiversity. By blurring boundaries between indoors and outdoors, our designs restore harmony between people and the natural world.

how
Every project begins with understanding—listening to visions, sensing the site, and learning from climate. Ideas then take shape, evolving into designs that respond with care. Finally, we guide the making, ensuring each space is built true to its intent.

**Examining Website Aesthetics**

I'm currently focused on the visual redesign of anvitam.com's main page. I've been heavily influenced by the design of blurringboundaries.in, using its content as a touchstone. I'm exploring how to translate those elements into a suitable layout for anvitam.com, considering the differences in branding and content. I'm prioritizing user experience and how to best showcase architectural work within this framework.


**Refining Visual Approach**

I'm now carefully mapping out the key sections from the reference site onto the anvitam.com framework. The "natural theme" request necessitates adapting the modern UI of the reference content. I'm focusing on the "What," "Why," and "Who" sections to understand the content flow. The goal is to retain the spirit of the reference site, but infuse a more organic, less sleek, visual identity, in line with feedback.


**Structuring Home Page Elements**

I'm deep into the home page redesign, focusing on a more natural aesthetic. I've begun integrating earthy colors into the `tailwind.config` to achieve a less modern feel. The focus is now on the content flow, translating the "What, Why, Who, How" sections into distinct parts. I'm prioritizing user experience with a focus on a simple hero section and a structured layout for project presentation. I'm exploring adding a scroll indicator to match the reference site.


**Outlining Page Flow**

I'm now concretizing the home page layout. I'm opting for a vertical stack or alternating structure for the "featured" feel. For the "Why" section, I'll use three columns or blocks for "Sustainable," "Contextual," and "Biophilic." The "How" section will get a simple 3-step visualization, while "Who" will introduce the founders. A simple list handles "Awards," and "Voices" will feature testimonials. The footer will conclude with a call to action. I am also working to adapt the existing navigation to reflect the core concepts or at least maintain functional links.


**Adjusting Site Navigation**

I'm now revising the navigation structure. I'm considering aligning the links with the "What, Why, Who" narrative. The current routes `/projects`, `/team`, and `/blog` need to be subtly adapted, perhaps using more natural styling. The reference site inspires me. I'll make the nav minimal, maybe transparent or off-white, to complement the design. Also, I'll update constants and project descriptions to fit the "natural" theme.


**Developing Home Page Sections**

I'm now fully immersed in coding the new home page in `pages/Home.tsx`, and I'm adding natural-feeling text colors like `text-[#2c2c2c]` instead of pure black for a more subtle look. The "What" section is now a dynamic project showcase, followed by the "Why" section with 3 distinct columns focusing on sustainability, context, and biophilic design. The "How" section, with its 3 visual steps, is also coming along. "Who" will lead to the Team, and a simple list of "Awards" will be ready soon, and I'm including the "Voices" slider. The footer will act as the call to action, and I'm updating the `index.html` file to add the custom color palette.


**Incorporating Feedback Insights**

I'm now revising my coding approach to better reflect a classic, natural aesthetic. I've updated `index.html` with a new background color. I'm focusing on "Awards" and "Testimonials" sections by updating `types.ts` and `constants.ts`. The home page will be the priority for this update, and I'll adapt the navigation styling in `components/Layout.tsx` and ensure admin functionality is retained. My primary focus is to get the home page aligned with the provided structure.


**Revising Homepage Strategy**

I'm now determining how to structure the home page and considering anchor links, given React Router. I'm choosing a summarized, scrollable home page design, linking to `/team` and `/projects`. The sections will include a Hero, "WHAT" showcase, "WHY" philosophy breakdown, "HOW" process steps, "WHO" founders, "AWARDS," "VOICES," and a CTA. The plan is to replace `Home.tsx` to match this structure.

To implement the design changes you requested, I will restructure the website to match the "Blurring Boundaries" aesthetic. This involves moving towards a "Natural" theme with warm earth tones, serif typography, and a specific content flow (What, Why, How, Who).

Here is the plan:
1.  **Update `index.html`**: Add a warm, natural color palette (`anvitam-cream`, `anvitam-charcoal`) to Tailwind.
2.  **Update `types.ts` & `constants.ts`**: Add data structures for **Awards** and **Testimonials** to populate the new home page sections.
3.  **Update `components/Layout.tsx`**: Redesign the navigation to be more minimal and classic, and update the footer to the "Let's build something together" style.
4.  **Update `pages/Home.tsx`**: Completely rewrite the landing page to follow the "What, Why, How, Who" narrative flow with the specific visual style you provided.

I'm currently focused on the color scheme shift for Anvitam's website. The existing cream and charcoal look is out, and I'm deeply immersed in exploring a nature-inspired palette. Greens and blues are at the forefront, aiming to achieve a harmonious and inviting aesthetic. I need to make sure the transition looks professional.


**Incorporating New Visuals**

The off-whites and light sage backgrounds will replace the current cream, enhancing the earthiness. Readability is key, so dark green or charcoal will be used for text. LAKE HOUSE, SURAT
The beauty of the structure is in the blend of materials and the play of light and shadow of the same. Designed by architects in Surat, the structure has landscape elements embedded both within and without, playing a very important role in enhancing the design. The green elements seen throughout the structure liven up the spaces and work to bring nature into the life of the user.

Blurring Boundaries, architects in Surat, Mumbai, craft a unique structure blending materials, light, shadow, and greenery to create a vibrant, nature-inspired space.

Maativan
The Lake house is a 4500 sq.ft 3-bedroom farmhouse, meant as a vacation home for a large family in Vesma, near Surat, Gujarat. It is set inside from the highway and overlooks a small artificial lake created by canal water towards the east and orchards in the west. The house is set slightly away from the compound wall and stands majestically against the lawn. The brick jail compound wall fades away into transparency to allow a view towards the lake from the house and the angular 3-dimensional main gate forms a strong statement with the angular roof of the house in the backdrop.
The first impression of the entrance of the farm house is the juxtaposition of the solid deep brick and the almost floating green cover on the metal frame. The visitor turns into the driveway with a garden view overflowing with bougainvillea flowers and parks underneath a vibrant canopy of creepers. The darkness in the entrance lobby beckons with the promise of coolness and respite from the hot sun. The visitor’s eye is drawn from the twisted column at the front to the circular arch in the wall behind. The sense of contrast between the brick or stone and the vibrant green is carried throughout the structure. When one enters the lobby, the brick arch further frames the stone arch which further frames the smaller circular brick arch, highlighted by the light in the court behind. The sense of depth in space and exploration is created and tantalises the visitor with curiosity. As he passes through the arch into the open verandah, there is a pause as one realises the visual textures in space on both sides. You see a landscaped pool on one side and the sunset through the green court on the other. Each side has a vista to offer and creates a small isolated traditional verandah for a group or individuals away from the rest of the structure.

The bamboo frame around the main door offers a glimpse into the main space, just hinting at more beyond the door. On entering the main door, there is a sense of soft light and yet the first visual is that of the pool with the backdrop of the brick wall. The plunge pool is a space to relax inside the living space for the family in the hot summers, while cooling down the interior space. It acts as a separate space from the main pool, introducing the possibility of 2 distinct groups also enjoying simultaneously, without creating walled barriers. The double height window with the view of the pool, the garden, the lake and the sky seamlessly merges the interiors with the outdoor. Mornings in the living space are greeted with a glow of east light and for the rest of the day; it is a pleasant space with soft brightness in all seasons. The double height bamboo roof gives the space warmth and the white IPS floor with the embedded Kota stone chips gives it an expanse. The twisted roof opens out towards the pool, creating a play of changing internal volumes and sense of it flowing outdoors. The pool is screened on the south east side from the sun and from the public entrance area with landscape, which extends towards the outdoor verandah between the entrance and the living space. The glimpses and connections of different spaces offer a sense of surprise and connect the various spaces.
The open kitchen, the court with the staircase, the plunge pool area and the pool with the outer deck and landscape are all extensions of the living and dining space. The spaces are all distinct from each other and demarcated using subtle play of the materials, roof, levels or barriers without defining each space. This gives it a lot of flexibility and a sense of being in a large seamless space, while maintaining integrity of the function and edges of each space. The play of the volume of space from the filler slab to the twisted bamboo ferro-cement roof naturally draws one’s eye to the sky beyond the tall glass facade, the pool and the lawn, seamlessly blending the indoors and outdoors.

The three courtyards connected to the living room and passages give a sense of light, shadow and openness to the central space. It does not feel enclosed since each stone or brick wall has an opening which gives a visual connection to the green and light in the courtyards. These also allow free cross ventilation through the spaces and make the farmhouse a pleasant space minimising or even eliminating the need for air-conditioning. 

The staircase with the leathered black granite with the exposed concrete folded plate profile sits in the central court and is lit up through the day from various angles, creating an interesting play of light and shadows. The Kota stone – rough and polished placed with the IPS floor adds a very subtle palette of shaded variations through the structure. 

The play of stone and the pots in the filler slabs or the bamboo and the brick wall along with the coloured IPS floor carries into the bedrooms, as in the public space. The bedrooms are each connected to the outdoor private gardens. The landscape is intended to disconnect from the main grounds and allow a blend of fragrance, flowers and beauty to surround the room. The cosiness of the room is welcome, when one enters the private space of the bedroom from the larger common spaces with large noisy and boisterous gatherings. 

The material palette responds to local availability and climate. The walls are load-bearing exposed brick, stone or lime plastered wall, creating a play of rich colours and textures. Brickwork is done in rat-trap-bond, saving material and keeping internal spaces cooler through insulation. The colourful IPS floors and the polished and unpolished Kota floors break the monotony of bricks and stones. The roof is used to divide spaces with flat filler exposed RCC slabs or sloping ferrocement slabs. The twisted Ferrocement roof with bamboo under-structure covering the living area and the bedrooms, allow for a variation of scale in the spaces. Locally sourced earthen pots are used in filler slabs, further increasing the comfort levels inside. 

Prashant Dupare and Shriya Parasrampuria were co-founders of istudio architecture along with a third partner previously. Lake house was part of their works at istudio architecture.

**Developing the Strategy**

I've been dissecting the request, pinpointing the key deliverables: clickable "How" items, distinct pages for "Understand," "Design," and "Deliver," and a detailed project section, alongside an update to the "Who" section. These are the core elements now. I am breaking this down further to identify the most efficient way to proceed. I am now evaluating the best flow for the user.


**Structuring the Content**

I've decided on the structure for the "How" section, opting for a dynamic routing solution using a single `Process.tsx` page to handle the "Understand," "Design," and "Deliver" sections.  I will be updating the "Who" section to match the provided text, including founder details, location, and taglines.  The key is to maintain a clean and effective user experience. I am currently focusing on the clickable project display and rich content implementation.


**Mapping the Routes**

I'm solidifying the routing strategy now. I've decided to go with a `/process/:phase` structure for the methodology sub-pages (Understand, Design, Deliver) for flexibility. The `/projects/:id` route for the project detail page will be dynamic, which I'm confident will be the right approach.  I'm now working to ensure the Lake House example content is accurately reflected within this new framework.


**Integrating Project Details**

I'm now integrating the "Lake House" project data, including updating the team member details to Archana Gavas. I'm focusing on the `ProjectDetail.tsx`, ensuring all specified components – title, location, hero image, gallery, and specs – are implemented for a comprehensive display. I'm working on the `Process.tsx` and adding placeholder text for the bullet points. I will update the "Who" section text and make sure the "How" section uses the new process pages in the `Home.tsx` component.


**Defining Project Structure**

I'm now focusing on the project data structure, specifically for the "Lake House" project. I'm adding `gallery` (array of strings) and `fullDescription`, `specs` to the `Project` type in `types.ts`, which I'll populate with the "Lake House" content. I am currently evaluating the most efficient method for managing placeholder images within the gallery.


**Adding Final Touches**

I'm now implementing the routing in `App.tsx` and updating the `Home.tsx` component to include the correct links to `/process/understand`, `/process/design`, and `/process/deliver`. In `pages/ProjectDetail.tsx`, I'm working on the design and layout, focusing on incorporating the "Blurring Boundaries" aesthetic with serif fonts, whitespace, and image grids. I have also added `Previous/Next` functionality to `ProjectDetail.tsx`. I am now updating `constants.ts` to include the Lake House project data, and `Process_Steps`. I need to ensure the "Who" text is copied correctly.


**Assembling Final Data**

I'm now generating the XML, ensuring consistency with placeholder images via `picsum` using specified seeds. The "Lake House" project, based in Surat, and team member Archana Gavas are integrated, and I am focused on making the "How" section on the Home page clickable as per design.

Here are the changes to implement the detailed "Who" section, the specific Process pages (Understand, Design, Deliver), and the rich Project Detail page template populated with the "Lake House" content.

payload.text:
**Updating Visualization Plans**

I'm currently focused on the testimonials, specifically adding images. I've been considering how to integrate these visually, potentially using placeholder imagery to maintain a clean aesthetic while still giving it a realistic feel. Removing the awards section is also on my mind, a change that requires a bit of code restructuring, but shouldn't be too complex.


**Adding Visual Enhancements**

I'm now thinking about the visual enhancements. First, I'll create a loading screen using the "Hamster Wheel" animation, integrating it globally in the app's initialization phase to enhance the user experience. Following that, I'll introduce a subtle, animated gradient background for the entire page, to achieve a "glass-like" aesthetic, without the backdrop-blur, since that's what's needed. This will involve adding a fixed background element in the layout.


**Finalizing Design Tweaks**

I've decided to refine the design further. I'm adding a semi-transparent layer in `Layout.tsx` or CSS to achieve a "glassy" appearance, making content easily readable. The testimonials will now include images, and I've updated `constants.ts` and `types.ts` accordingly. I'll remove the awards section. I'll add the necessary CSS for the hamster loading animation to the `<style>` block in `index.html`. Finally, I'll update `types.ts` to include the `image` field in `Testimonial`, and then update `constants.ts` to add image URLs.


**Implementing New Effects**

I'm now integrating new effects. I've added CSS for the hamster wheel and a flowing gradient background to `index.html`. A `Loader` component is created, displaying for 2-3 seconds on initial load, and the Awards section is removed from `Home.tsx`. Finally, I'm adjusting the background styling in `Layout.tsx` and adding a fixed, animated gradient background in `index.html` to achieve the "glass" effect. I'm using `bg-anvitam-cream/90` on `Layout` to allow the user's intent of the glass design to come through.


**Implementing Visuals Further**

I'm now diving into the code. I'm updating `types.ts` and `constants.ts` to integrate images with testimonials. I've added the hamster wheel and gradient animation CSS to `index.html`, and I'm creating a `Loader` component.  I've adjusted the `App.tsx` and `Home.tsx` files. I'm focusing on "glassy" styling. The plan is to set the body's background to the animated gradient and use `bg-anvitam-cream/80 backdrop-blur-xl` on the `Layout` to create a strong glass effect. I'll pick random user images for testimonials initially.


**Preparing CSS Implementation**

I'm now focusing on integrating the proposed CSS code, carefully copying it into `index.html`. My aim is to use placeholder images that appear realistic for testimonials. I'll remove the Awards section from `Home.tsx`. For the glass effect, I'm adapting the gradient to a nature-themed color scheme, using a soft gradient of cream, green, and blue, instead of the classic gradient. I'll begin preparing the code, and then implement.

To satisfy your request, I will:

1.  **Add the "Hamster Wheel" Loading Screen**: I'll create a loader component with the specific CSS you provided and display it when the website first opens.
2.  **Add a "Glass" & Animated Gradient Background**: I'll add a flowing, nature-inspired gradient animation to the background and make the layout semi-transparent with a blur effect (glassmorphism) so the movement is visible behind the content.
3.  **Update "Voices" (Testimonials)**: I will add images to the testimonials section.
4.  **Remove the Awards Section**: I will delete this section from the Home page.

Here are the changes:

  }

export const INITIAL_PROJECTS: Project[] = [
  {
    id: 'lake-house',
    title: 'Lake House',
    category: 'Residential',
    location: 'Surat, Gujarat',
    year: '2023',
    image: 'https://picsum.photos/1200/800?random=10',
    description: 'The beauty of the structure is in the blend of materials and the play of light and shadow.',
    fullDescription: `The Lake house is a 4500 sq.ft 3-bedroom farmhouse, meant as a vacation home for a large family in Vesma, near Surat, Gujarat. It is set inside from the highway and overlooks a small artificial lake created by canal water towards the east and orchards in the west. The house is set slightly away from the compound wall and stands majestically against the lawn. The brick jail compound wall fades away into transparency to allow a view towards the lake from the house and the angular 3-dimensional main gate forms a strong statement with the angular roof of the house in the backdrop.

    The first impression of the entrance of the farm house is the juxtaposition of the solid deep brick and the almost floating green cover on the metal frame. The visitor turns into the driveway with a garden view overflowing with bougainvillea flowers and parks underneath a vibrant canopy of creepers. The darkness in the entrance lobby beckons with the promise of coolness and respite from the hot sun. The visitor’s eye is drawn from the twisted column at the front to the circular arch in the wall behind. The sense of contrast between the brick or stone and the vibrant green is carried throughout the structure. When one enters the lobby, the brick arch further frames the stone arch which further frames the smaller circular brick arch, highlighted by the light in the court behind. The sense of depth in space and exploration is created and tantalises the visitor with curiosity. As he passes through the arch into the open verandah, there is a pause as one realises the visual textures in space on both sides. You see a landscaped pool on one side and the sunset through the green court on the other. Each side has a vista to offer and creates a small isolated traditional verandah for a group or individuals away from the rest of the structure.

    The bamboo frame around the main door offers a glimpse into the main space, just hinting at more beyond the door. On entering the main door, there is a sense of soft light and yet the first visual is that of the pool with the backdrop of the brick wall. The plunge pool is a space to relax inside the living space for the family in the hot summers, while cooling down the interior space. It acts as a separate space from the main pool, introducing the possibility of 2 distinct groups also enjoying simultaneously, without creating walled barriers. The double height window with the view of the pool, the garden, the lake and the sky seamlessly merges the interiors with the outdoor. Mornings in the living space are greeted with a glow of east light and for the rest of the day; it is a pleasant space with soft brightness in all seasons. The double height bamboo roof gives the space warmth and the white IPS floor with the embedded Kota stone chips gives it an expanse. The twisted roof opens out towards the pool, creating a play of changing internal volumes and sense of it flowing outdoors. The pool is screened on the south east side from the sun and from the public entrance area with landscape, which extends towards the outdoor verandah between the entrance and the living space. The glimpses and connections of different spaces offer a sense of surprise and connect the various spaces.
    
    The open kitchen, the court with the staircase, the plunge pool area and the pool with the outer deck and landscape are all extensions of the living and dining space. The spaces are all distinct from each other and demarcated using subtle play of the materials, roof, levels or barriers without defining each space. This gives it a lot of flexibility and a sense of being in a large seamless space, while maintaining integrity of the function and edges of each space. The play of the volume of space from the filler slab to the twisted bamboo ferro-cement roof naturally draws one’s eye to the sky beyond the tall glass facade, the pool and the lawn, seamlessly blending the indoors and outdoors.

    The three courtyards connected to the living room and passages give a sense of light, shadow and openness to the central space. It does not feel enclosed since each stone or brick wall has an opening which gives a visual connection to the green and light in the courtyards. These also allow free cross ventilation through the spaces and make the farmhouse a pleasant space minimising or even eliminating the need for air-conditioning. 
    
    The staircase with the leathered black granite with the exposed concrete folded plate profile sits in the central court and is lit up through the day from various angles, creating an interesting play of light and shadows. The Kota stone – rough and polished placed with the IPS floor adds a very subtle palette of shaded variations through the structure. 
    
    The play of stone and the pots in the filler slabs or the bamboo and the brick wall along with the coloured IPS floor carries into the bedrooms, as in the public space. The bedrooms are each connected to the outdoor private gardens. The landscape is intended to disconnect from the main grounds and allow a blend of fragrance, flowers and beauty to surround the room. The cosiness of the room is welcome, when one enters the private space of the bedroom from the larger common spaces with large noisy and boisterous gatherings. 
    
    The material palette responds to local availability and climate. The walls are load-bearing exposed brick, stone or lime plastered wall, creating a play of rich colours and textures. Brickwork is done in rat-trap-bond, saving material and keeping internal spaces cooler through insulation. The colourful IPS floors and the polished and unpolished Kota floors break the monotony of bricks and stones. The roof is used to divide spaces with flat filler exposed RCC slabs or sloping ferrocement slabs. The twisted Ferrocement roof with bamboo under-structure covering the living area and the bedrooms, allow for a variation of scale in the spaces. Locally sourced earthen pots are used in filler slabs, further increasing the comfort levels inside.`,
    gallery: [
      'https://picsum.photos/1200/800?random=11',
      'https://picsum.photos/800/1200?random=12',

    ],

    ],

  },

    year: '2023',
    image: 'https://picsum.photos/1200/800?random=1',

    fullDescription: 'Full description pending...',

need previous next t oread in the blog so peoeol cna toggle when reading also the imaes that we add in prject shoud hoer sans dee detisl like interor , bedreoome tc so reder eg ino that waht image si reprseting in projects

in who i need to add detila bout the fouder a bit like this [team
Shriya Parasrampuria and Prashant Dupare are the founders and principal architects of Blurring Boundaries, a contextualist design practice in Mumbai, engaged in creating sustainable and appropriate biophilic design solutions. They are 2007 graduates from Sir JJ college of architecture, Mumbai and have had an architectural practice for more than 14 years. They have worked on a plethora of residential projects, hospitality projects, educational buildings, with ecological materials and techniques.

Meet us!
AboutAbout
I didn’t start with a firm.

No team. No funding. No roadmap.

Just a quiet belief —

that design could be more than aesthetic.

It could be healing. Grounding. Human centric.

As a freelance architect, I wore every hat.

➝ from concept to execution
➝ from budgeting to supervision
➝ from client calls to creative blocks

What kept me going?

The hope that every space I touched
might help someone feel seen, safe, or inspired.

That belief shaped Anvitam —
my independent architecture studio, built from the ground up.

I’ve led 10+ projects — spanning

➝ residential architecture
➝ commercial space design
➝ community-driven architecture
➝ interior & exterior transformations
➝ landscape and permaculture planning

Each one rooted in context, emotion, and honest simplicity.

But creativity, to me, doesn’t end at walls and plans.

It belongs in classrooms, offices, and playgrounds too.

That’s why I founded Nest N Nurture —
a space for creative workshops that bring imagination back into design.

We invite people — especially children — to transform their own spaces
using color, stories, and collaboration.

It’s not just a makeover.

It’s ownership.

It’s joyful design with purpose.

My process is Raw. Intentional.
Rooted in permaculture, nature-based systems, and user-centered thinking.

Whether it’s a garden, a classroom, or a living room —
I design for how people feel, grow, and belong in a space.

Yes, the road’s been rough.

Being a solo woman founder in architecture comes with its weight.

But I’ve learned to trust the quiet power of consistency, intuition, and care.

I don’t follow trends.

I follow stories. People. Place. Possibility.

If you believe design can be deeper —

let’s build something meaningful together.Email: anvitamarchitect@gmail.com]

also  wrtiw why page like this - about us
Blurring boundaries, architects in Mumbai, India is contextual architecture and interior design firm practicing sustainable, eco-friendly biophilic designs that blends seamlessly with nature. We create contemporary spaces using local materials and craftsmanship, thoughtfully combined with traditional and modern technologies.

Belief
Our practice is artisanal, experimental, and collaborative, grounded in sustainable and “glocal” values. We care equally for fine details and the broader vision, thoughtfully crafting spaces that are both responsive and rooted.

We believe design can transform the way we live, and must be approached with sensitivity, purpose, and responsibility. Sustainability is not just a goal, but a mindset that shapes every decision. Biophilic Design: Connecting Architecture with Nature

Biophilic Design in India: Sustainable Architecture Rooted in Nature
Conclusion
What is biophilic design
Biophilic design is an architectural approach that integrates natural elements—such as light, air, greenery, and organic materials—into built environments to promote well-being, productivity, and a deeper connection to nature.

Rooted in the belief that humans have an innate connection to nature, biophilic design incorporates natural elements, materials, and processes to create spaces that promote well-being, enhance productivity, and contribute to environmental sustainability. This approach is not just about aesthetics—it fundamentally improves the quality of life for occupants by fostering a closer connection to the natural world.

Biophilic architecture

 
Principles of Biophilic Design
Stephen Kellert, a pioneer in biophilic design, identified six core elements that guide its application:

1. Environmental Features of biophilic design
This principle emphasizes the direct integration of natural elements into the built environment. Incorporating plants, water features, sunlight, fresh air, and natural materials such as wood and stone creates spaces that resonate with human affinity for nature.

Attributes:
Use of plants and greenery
Sunlight and well-ventilated spaces
Water features and natural materials
Views of natural landscapes
2. Natural Shapes and Forms
Biophilic architecture employs shapes and patterns found in nature—such as curves, arches, and organic forms—to evoke a sense of harmony.

Attributes:
Botanical motifs and biomorphic forms
Curved and irregular shapes
Structures resembling natural forms (e.g., tree columns, shell-like domes)
3. Natural Patterns and Processes
This element focuses on incorporating natural variability, richness of detail, and sensory experiences. Patterns in materials, changing light conditions, and textured surfaces mimic the dynamic processes found in nature.

Attributes:
Sensory variability (light, sound, textures)
Dynamic lighting and shading
Central focal points and patterned wholes
4. Light and Space
Natural light plays a pivotal role in biophilic architecture. Spaces are designed to maximize daylight, creating a vibrant environment that changes throughout the day.

Attributes:
Use of natural and filtered light
Creation of light pools and reflected light effects
Spaciousness and spatial variability
Inside-outside connectivity
5. Place-Based Relationships
Biophilic design fosters a sense of place by drawing inspiration from local culture, geography, and ecology. This connection enhances the uniqueness of each project and promotes sustainability through the use of indigenous materials.

Attributes:
Use of locally sourced materials
Integration of cultural and ecological elements
Preservation of site’s natural topography and vegetation
6. Evolved Human-Nature Relationships
This principle acknowledges the complex emotional and psychological relationship humans have with nature. Spaces are designed to balance safety with exploration, fostering curiosity, awe, and a sense of belonging.

Attributes:
Prospect and refuge
Exploration and discovery
Security and protection
Attraction and beauty
biophilic office design workspace

Biophilia in urban architecture

 
Benefits of Biophilic Design
 

1. Enhances Mental Health and Well-being
Being surrounded by natural elements has been shown to reduce stress, anxiety, and depression. The calming effect of greenery, natural light, and organic textures creates a soothing environment that helps individuals feel more relaxed and grounded.

 
2. Boosts Productivity and Creativity
Biophilic design enhances focus, productivity, and creativity in workplaces, schools, and other environments. Natural lighting, access to greenery, and outdoor views stimulate the mind and help reduce mental fatigue.

3. Promotes Physical Health
By improving air quality and incorporating natural ventilation, biophilic architecture can enhance physical health. Plants help purify the air, while natural ventilation systems reduce the buildup of indoor pollutants.

4. Strengthens Connection to Nature
In urban settings, people are often disconnected from nature, which can lead to a range of mental and physical health issues. Biophilic design helps restore this connection by bringing elements of the natural world into the built environment.

 
5. Enhances Learning Environments
Biophilic architecture in educational spaces has been linked to better learning outcomes. Exposure to nature helps improve students’ concentration, memory, and overall engagement in learning.

6. Improves Social Interaction
Biophilic design fosters community and social interaction by creating inviting communal spaces. People naturally gravitate toward green, well-lit areas, making them ideal for social gatherings.

7. Encourages Sustainable Living
Biophilic architecture is inherently sustainable. By integrating renewable energy sources, water management systems, and green roofs, biophilic buildings reduce their ecological footprint.

8. Enhances Acoustic Comfort
Natural materials and features such as wooden surfaces, water bodies, and green walls help absorb sound, reducing noise pollution and enhancing acoustic comfort in indoor environments.

9. Supports Biodiversity
By integrating plant life and natural ecosystems, biophilic buildings contribute to local biodiversity. Green roofs, vertical gardens, and native landscaping support pollinators and other wildlife.

 

biophilic-architecture-design-nature

 

 
Implementing Biophilic Design in Practice
 
1. Incorporating Greenery
Introduce plants and greenery both indoors and outdoors. Vertical gardens, green roofs, and indoor plants enhance air quality, reduce heat, and provide a calming atmosphere.

 
2. Maximizing Natural Light
Design spaces to allow ample daylight, using large windows, skylights, and light wells. Use shading devices to control glare and heat while maintaining a visual connection to the outside.

 
3. Using Natural Materials
Choose materials with low embodied energy and a natural aesthetic, such as wood, stone, bamboo, and terracotta. Locally sourced materials further reduce the environmental footprint.

 
4. Creating Fluid Indoor-Outdoor Transitions
Design spaces that blur the boundaries between indoor and outdoor environments. Courtyards, patios, and large openings foster a seamless connection with nature.

 
5. Integrating Water Features
Water features such as ponds, fountains, and waterfalls add an element of tranquility while also improving microclimate and humidity.

 
6. Designing for Sensory Richness
Incorporate diverse textures, colors, and patterns inspired by nature. Use soundscapes and olfactory elements to engage multiple senses.

Biophilia in Interiors
Biophilia shapes not just our architecture but also our interior design philosophy. Across several homes and retreats near Mumbai, we’ve crafted interiors that invite nature inside — through material, light, form, and sensory experience.

We use natural finishes like lime plaster, reclaimed wood, and earth-based flooring to create tactile warmth. Indoor courtyards, skylights, and large openings bring in sunlight, shadows, and breeze, fostering a constant dialogue between inside and out.

Rather than treating interiors as sealed environments, we see them as evolving ecosystems — where human comfort, natural rhythms, and spatial aesthetics coexist. This integration of biophilia within interiors enhances well-being, reduces stress, and creates spaces that feel grounded, alive, and emotionally rich.

090
biophilic architecture design
A walk through our biophilic project near Mumbai - Maativan

At Blurring Boundaries, biophilic design is at the heart of many of our eco-conscious retreats near Mumbai. By integrating natural elements — like building around trees, maximizing daylight, and using materials like mud, stone, and lime — we create spaces that foster a deep connection with nature.

In projects like Maativan, the forest guided the architecture — resulting in organically flowing forms, shaded courtyards, and tree-canopied roofs that promote thermal comfort and sensory richness. This approach doesn’t just reduce environmental impact; it enhances well-being.

Like the philosophies of Nari Gandhi and Geoffrey Bawa, our biophilic design approach blends ancient wisdom with modern techniques — crafting spaces that are sustainable, climatically responsive, and emotionally resonant.

Biophilic Design in India: Sustainable Architecture Rooted in Nature
India has a rich architectural history that has always embraced nature — from the open courtyards of havelis and stepwells to the shaded pavilions and verandahs of traditional homes. Biophilic design builds on this heritage, offering a contemporary approach that re-establishes the bond between people and the natural world through architecture and interior design.

In the Indian context, biophilic design is more than just aesthetics — it’s a response to climate, culture, and ecology. It involves the thoughtful use of natural ventilation, daylighting, local materials like stone, clay, and wood, and the integration of green elements such as plants, water features, and shaded outdoor areas. These elements not only reduce energy use but also create environments that support physical and mental well-being.

Given India’s diverse climate zones, biophilic strategies can vary — from cool, earthen architecture in the hot and dry regions to lush, rain-adapted forms in tropical zones. Yet the core idea remains the same: to create spaces that feel alive, human, and deeply connected to their surroundings.

By combining traditional knowledge systems with modern design thinking, biophilic design in India is emerging as a sustainable and culturally relevant approach to building for the future.

Conclusion
In an age of rapid urbanisation, digital saturation, and environmental stress, biophilic design offers more than just aesthetic relief — it offers a return to balance. By weaving nature into the fabric of our homes, workspaces, and community spaces, we create environments that not only conserve resources but also nurture the human spirit.

Biophilia reminds us that well-being is deeply tied to our connection with the natural world. Whether through daylight filtering into a room, a tree growing through a courtyard, or materials that breathe and age with grace — these quiet, intentional gestures can transform how we feel, heal, and live.

At Blurring Boundaries, we see biophilic design not as a style, but as a responsibility — to design spaces that are alive, responsive, and deeply human. Because in reconnecting with nature, we ultimately reconnect with ourselves.

{/* Left Col: Image & Contact */}

Archana Gavas

Principal Architect, Founder

anvitamarchitect@gmail.com

LinkedIn Profile
{/* Right Col: Narrative */}

Meet Us

"I didn’t start with a firm. No team. No funding. No roadmap. Just a quiet belief."

As a freelance architect, I wore every hat. From concept to execution, budgeting to supervision. What kept me going was the hope that every space I touched might help someone feel seen, safe, or inspired.

That belief shaped Anvitam — my independent architecture studio, built from the ground up. I’ve led 10+ projects spanning residential architecture, commercial space design, community-driven architecture, and landscape planning. Each one rooted in context, emotion, and honest simplicity.

But creativity, to me, doesn’t end at walls and plans. It belongs in classrooms, offices, and playgrounds too. That’s why I founded Nest N Nurture — a space for creative workshops that bring imagination back into design. We invite people — especially children — to transform their own spaces using color, stories, and collaboration. It’s not just a makeover. It’s ownership.

"My process is Raw. Intentional. Rooted in permaculture, nature-based systems, and user-centered thinking."

Whether it’s a garden, a classroom, or a living room — I design for how people feel, grow, and belong in a space.

Yes, the road’s been rough. Being a solo woman founder in architecture comes with its weight. But I’ve learned to trust the quiet power of consistency, intuition, and care. I don’t follow trends. I follow stories. People. Place. Possibility.

If you believe design can be deeper — let’s build something meaningful together.

); }; export default Team;

export const INITIAL_BLOGS: BlogPost[] = [ { id: 'biophilic-design', title: 'Biophilic Design: Connecting Architecture with Nature', date: 'October 25, 2023', author: 'Anvitam Team', excerpt: 'Rooted in the belief that humans have an innate connection to nature, biophilic design incorporates natural elements to create spaces that promote well-being.', image: 'https://picsum.photos/1200/600?random=100', toc: [ 'What is biophilic design', 'Principles of Biophilic Design', 'Benefits of Biophilic Design', 'Implementing Biophilic Design', 'Comparison: Conventional vs Biophilic', 'Biophilia in Interiors' ], content: `

What is biophilic design

Biophilic design is an architectural approach that integrates natural elements—such as light, air, greenery, and organic materials—into built environments to promote well-being, productivity, and a deeper connection to nature.

Rooted in the belief that humans have an innate connection to nature, biophilic design incorporates natural elements, materials, and processes to create spaces that promote well-being, enhance productivity, and contribute to environmental sustainability. This approach is not just about aesthetics—it fundamentally improves the quality of life for occupants by fostering a closer connection to the natural world.

Principles of Biophilic Design

Stephen Kellert, a pioneer in biophilic design, identified six core elements that guide its application:

1. Environmental Features

This principle emphasizes the direct integration of natural elements into the built environment. Incorporating plants, water features, sunlight, fresh air, and natural materials such as wood and stone creates spaces that resonate with human affinity for nature.

2. Natural Shapes and Forms

Biophilic architecture employs shapes and patterns found in nature—such as curves, arches, and organic forms—to evoke a sense of harmony.

3. Natural Patterns and Processes

This element focuses on incorporating natural variability, richness of detail, and sensory experiences. Patterns in materials, changing light conditions, and textured surfaces mimic the dynamic processes found in nature.

Benefits of Biophilic Design

  1. Enhances Mental Health: Being surrounded by natural elements has been shown to reduce stress, anxiety, and depression.
  2. Boosts Productivity: Natural lighting and greenery stimulate the mind and reduce fatigue.
  3. Promotes Physical Health: Improved air quality and natural ventilation.

Comparison: Conventional Design vs Biophilic Design

Feature Conventional Design Biophilic Design
Connection to Nature Minimal or incidental 100% Integrated
Materials Used Often synthetic/industrial Natural, Local, Earthy
User Well-being Not always considered Central to design

Biophilia in Interiors

Biophilia shapes not just our architecture but also our interior design philosophy. Across several homes and retreats near Mumbai, we’ve crafted interiors that invite nature inside — through material, light, form, and sensory experience.

We use natural finishes like lime plaster, reclaimed wood, and earth-based flooring to create tactile warmth. Indoor courtyards, skylights, and large openings bring in sunlight, shadows, and breeze, fostering a constant dialogue between inside and out.

Conclusion

In an age of rapid urbanisation, biophilic design offers a return to balance. By weaving nature into the fabric of our homes, we create environments that nurture the human spirit. At Anvitam, we see biophilic design not as a style, but as a responsibility.

export const INITIAL_BLOGS: BlogPost[] = [ { id: 'biophilic-design', title: 'Biophilic Design: Connecting Architecture with Nature', date: 'October 25, 2023', author: 'Anvitam Team', excerpt: 'Rooted in the belief that humans have an innate connection to nature, biophilic design incorporates natural elements to create spaces that promote well-being.', image: 'https://picsum.photos/1200/600?random=100', toc: [ 'What is biophilic design', 'Principles of Biophilic Design', 'Benefits of Biophilic Design', 'Implementing Biophilic Design', 'Comparison: Conventional vs Biophilic', 'Biophilia in Interiors' ], content: `

What is biophilic design

Biophilic design is an architectural approach that integrates natural elements—such as light, air, greenery, and organic materials—into built environments to promote well-being, productivity, and a deeper connection to nature.

Rooted in the belief that humans have an innate connection to nature, biophilic design incorporates natural elements, materials, and processes to create spaces that promote well-being, enhance productivity, and contribute to environmental sustainability. This approach is not just about aesthetics—it fundamentally improves the quality of life for occupants by fostering a closer connection to the natural world.

Principles of Biophilic Design

Stephen Kellert, a pioneer in biophilic design, identified six core elements that guide its application:

1. Environmental Features

This principle emphasizes the direct integration of natural elements into the built environment. Incorporating plants, water features, sunlight, fresh air, and natural materials such as wood and stone creates spaces that resonate with human affinity for nature.

2. Natural Shapes and Forms

Biophilic architecture employs shapes and patterns found in nature—such as curves, arches, and organic forms—to evoke a sense of harmony.

3. Natural Patterns and Processes

This element focuses on incorporating natural variability, richness of detail, and sensory experiences. Patterns in materials, changing light conditions, and textured surfaces mimic the dynamic processes found in nature.

Benefits of Biophilic Design

  1. Enhances Mental Health: Being surrounded by natural elements has been shown to reduce stress, anxiety, and depression.
  2. Boosts Productivity: Natural lighting and greenery stimulate the mind and reduce fatigue.
  3. Promotes Physical Health: Improved air quality and natural ventilation.

Comparison: Conventional Design vs Biophilic Design

Feature Conventional Design Biophilic Design
Connection to Nature Minimal or incidental 100% Integrated
Materials Used Often synthetic/industrial Natural, Local, Earthy
User Well-being Not always considered Central to design

Biophilia in Interiors

Biophilia shapes not just our architecture but also our interior design philosophy. Across several homes and retreats near Mumbai, we’ve crafted interiors that invite nature inside — through material, light, form, and sensory experience.

We use natural finishes like lime plaster, reclaimed wood, and earth-based flooring to create tactile warmth. Indoor courtyards, skylights, and large openings bring in sunlight, shadows, and breeze, fostering a constant dialogue between inside and out.

Conclusion

In an age of rapid urbanisation, biophilic design offers a return to balance. By weaving nature into the fabric of our homes, we create environments that nurture the human spirit. At Anvitam, we see biophilic design not as a style, but as a responsibility.

` }, { id: 'future-sustainable', title: 'The Future of Sustainable Architecture in Gujarat', date: 'October 15, 2023', author: 'Archana Gavas', excerpt: 'Exploring how traditional Gujarati building techniques can be adapted for modern sustainability needs.', content: '

Full article content regarding sustainable practices in Gujarat...

Archana doesn't just build—she listens, deeply.", author: 'Kapil Sharma', role: 'Founder, Ramisera Wilds', image: 'https://picsum.photos/100/100?random=50' }, { id: 'tm2', text: "As an Ayurvedic doctor, I wanted a home in sync with nature. Anvitam understood that completely. import React from 'react'; import { TEAM_MEMBERS } from '../constants'; const Team: React.FC = () => { const archana = TEAM_MEMBERS.find(m => m.name.includes('Archana')); return (
{/* Left Col: Image & Contact */}
{ e.currentTarget.src = "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1573496359142-b8d87734a5a2?q=80&w=600&auto=format&fit=crop"; // Fallback }} />

Archana Gavas

Principal Architect, Founder

anvitamarchitect@gmail.com

LinkedIn Profile
{/* Right Col: Narrative */}

Meet Us

"I didn’t start with a firm. No team. No funding. No roadmap. Just a quiet belief."

As a freelance architect, I wore every hat. From concept to execution, budgeting to supervision. What kept me going was the hope that every space I touched might help someone feel seen, safe, or inspired.

That belief shaped Anvitam — my independent architecture studio, built from the ground up. I’ve led 10+ projects spanning residential architecture, commercial space design, community-driven architecture, and landscape planning. Each one rooted in context, emotion, and honest simplicity.

But creativity, to me, doesn’t end at walls and plans. It belongs in classrooms, offices, and playgrounds too. That’s why I founded Nest N Nurture — a space for creative workshops that bring imagination back into design. We invite people — especially children — to transform their own spaces using color, stories, and collaboration. It’s not just a makeover. It’s ownership.

"My process is Raw. Intentional. Rooted in permaculture, nature-based systems, and user-centered thinking."

Whether it’s a garden, a classroom, or a living room — I design for how people feel, grow, and belong in a space.

Yes, the road’s been rough. Being a solo woman founder in architecture comes with its weight. But I’ve learned to trust the quiet power of consistency, intuition, and care. I don’t follow trends. I follow stories. People. Place. Possibility.

If you believe design can be deeper — let’s build something meaningful together.

); }; export default Team;

payload.text:
,fix my color patter please - 
#FAF6E9 - primary 
#DDEB9D - 
#A0C878

**Revising Color Choices**

I've been meticulously reviewing the current color selections for the website. The primary, a creamy `#FAF6E9`, feels solid. However, I'm now reconsidering the secondary palette options. The initial light yellowish-green, `#DDEB9D`, seems a bit muted. I'm exploring bolder alternatives to create a more dynamic and engaging visual experience. I'm thinking about how the tone can be created.


**Mapping Color Updates**

I'm currently focused on integrating the new accent color, `#A0C878`, into the existing Tailwind configuration. I've analyzed the current `index.html` file and identified the `anvitam-*` color mappings. The challenge is deciding where to place this new green. I'm considering replacing either `anvitam-stone` or `anvitam-blue` to accommodate this new tone. I am debating between two of the secondary colors as a placement. This requires careful consideration to ensure a balanced aesthetic. I've got to find the right color placement!


**Finalizing Color Mapping**

I've decided to keep the original `anvitam-charcoal` for text readability, avoiding overly dark green options. The new `#A0C878` will replace the existing `anvitam-green` for accents, highlighting, and leaf imagery.  I'll map the lighter `#DDEB9D` to a new `anvitam-lightgreen` to use as a background offset, instead of modifying the existing `anvitam-stone` or `anvitam-blue` to avoid color scheme disruptions. This ensures a balanced and readable palette for the website.


**Updating Color Scheme Decisions**

payload.text:
In the "Why" page, add images Please, with each section, make reder feel a premium vibe—why we design
"Anvitam is contextual architecture and interior design practicing sustainable, eco-friendly biophilic designs that blends seamlessly with nature."

01. Belief
Artisanal. Experimental. Collaborative.
We care equally for fine details and the broader vision, thoughtfully crafting spaces that are both responsive and rooted. We believe design can transform the way we live, and must be approached with sensitivity, purpose, and responsibility.

Sustainability is not just a goal, but a mindset that shapes every decision. We often use locally available materials, reinterpreting them to create contemporary expressions that are both relevant and timeless.

Architecture, to us, is a fluid relationship between the physical, psychological, social, and cultural. Through biophilic design, we create spaces that blur the lines between built form and natural context. Our work embraces contrast, translating human complexity into spatial clarity.

02. Philosophy
Shaping Meaningful Spaces
We are a young, reflective practice — playful yet deeply grounded in the responsibility of shaping meaningful spaces. Our philosophy is to blend design, materials, and technology into seamless everyday experiences.

Every project is a story of its own, shaped by its site, climate, material, labor, and budget. Context is central — it helps prevent problems and reveals opportunities. We begin by understanding the user’s intent, needs, and long-term vision. From there, we build design solutions through a clear and thoughtful process.

Our work is never formulaic. Each design evolves from listening — to the people, the land, and the pulse of the project. We treat every project as an individual expression, with care for both conceptual depth and real-world function.

03. Approach
Care for the Earth & Craft
Our approach begins with care — for the earth, for people, and for the craft of architecture. We focus on ecological practices and sustainable thinking, considering the health and environmental impact of design choices.

We believe in reconnecting people to nature through thoughtful, biophilic spaces. Our buildings aim to integrate, not isolate — offering comfort, awareness, and beauty. An experimental mindset allows us to question norms and innovate in simple, human-centered ways.

We find joy in the design process — a mix of curiosity, creativity, and playful exploration. Design doesn’t always have to be serious; it can be light, meaningful, and fun. Collaboration with communities, craftsmen, and creative minds is essential to our process — it leads to richer, more layered outcomes and keeps our work evolving.

**Updating the Philosophy Page**

I'm currently focused on the `Why` page, specifically the `pages/Why.tsx` file. The aim is to enhance its visual appeal by integrating images into the 'Belief', 'Philosophy', and 'Approach' sections. The text content seems accurate, aligning with the existing content, and I'm reviewing to ensure smooth integration. The goal is a premium, high-quality feel.


**Designing the Why Page**

I'm now diving into the design phase for the `Why` page. The plan involves alternating text and images to establish a premium editorial aesthetic. I'm focusing on acquiring high-quality visuals and have identified potential sources like `picsum.photos` and `unsplash`. Each section will have a corresponding image: craftsmanship for "Belief," spatial depth for "Philosophy," and nature/sustainability for "Approach." I'm aiming for a clean aesthetic with cream or white backgrounds, using large impactful images with varied aspect ratios, and considering subtle animations like parallax or fade-ins to enhance visual appeal.


**Reviewing Implementation Details**

The `pages/Why.tsx` file is under scrutiny. The plan is to broaden the container from `max-w-4xl` to a `max-w-7xl` or adopt a grid system for the premium layout with side-by-side images. I'm contemplating how to present the header, possibly with a subtle background or hero image, aiming for emphasis on the quote. For the "Belief" section, I'm structuring a two-column layout on desktop, with text on the left and an image on the right.


**Implementing the Visuals**

I'm now integrating visuals for the `Why` page, starting with the "Belief" section. I've chosen an abstract texture image from Unsplash to convey craftsmanship, aiming for a warm, artisanal feel. For "Philosophy," I'm considering modern architectural shots, like a bright, airy home interior. The goal is to create a cohesive aesthetic, and Unsplash allows for much better quality. I'll test these on the page to judge the effect!


**Choosing Visuals for Sections**

I've selected final images for the "Approach" section, favoring the interior with plants from Unsplash for its biophilic theme. I'll focus on implementing these selections to enhance visual coherence with the other sections. Now, I'm keen to implement these images into the existing design.


**Implementing the Text Content**

I'm now integrating the text content into the Why page, and it's taking shape. I'm focusing on the first section, "Belief," as the images are already implemented. It's a two-column grid on desktop, text and image. The text introduces the philosophy, emphasizing craftsmanship, experimentation, and collaboration. I'm aiming for smooth formatting and good readability in the layout.


**Refining Section Content**

The "Philosophy" section is now receiving my attention. I've integrated the architectural images. I'm focusing on the interplay of the images and text to communicate the company philosophy, emphasizing the fluid relationship between physical, psychological, social, and cultural aspects. I’m working on making the page flow seamlessly from section to section.


**Adjusting Section Layout**

I'm currently working on the "Philosophy" section layout, focusing on the interplay between text and the image. The goal is to achieve visual balance and create a seamless flow with the surrounding content, using a responsive design. I'm considering fine-tuning the spacing, padding and positioning to make the page more visually appealing. I'm also ensuring a consistent brand aesthetic.


**Crafting the "Approach" Section**

I'm now integrating the text for the "Approach" section, focusing on crafting a compelling narrative for the existing content. My aim is to convey the practice's values – blending design, materials, and technology into seamless experiences. I'm highlighting the importance of context in shaping each project as a unique story, and emphasizing how listening to the various project elements drives the design process.


**Polishing Section Aesthetics**

I'm presently focusing on the "Approach" section's visual elements, adjusting image placement and text flow. The goal is to enhance readability, ensuring that the existing content describing our sustainable and biophilic approach resonates. Now I'm refining the image and text alignment to highlight the practice's values.


**Adding Visual Refinement**

I'm now in the process of integrating specific visual refinements into the `Why` page, focusing on the details of image presentation and layout elements. I'm aiming for smooth transitions, subtle animations where appropriate, and ensure visual coherence across the page. I'm focusing on the overall user experience and aesthetic appeal.


**Completing the Integration**

I'm finalizing the images for the "Why" page. I've chosen an artisanal texture for "Belief," a modern architectural interior for "Philosophy," and a biophilic interior for "Approach" from Unsplash. I'm focusing on the image placement and ensuring that the text content flows well around the visuals. The background color of the layout is cream, which will make the images stand out. I'll test the page once I finish the implementation.


**Testing Image Placement**

I'm now testing the image placement, ensuring that the selected images are visually impactful and maintain a cohesive narrative throughout the page. I've begun to integrate the visuals and am assessing how well the chosen images complement the text content, and each other. I'm focusing on ensuring consistent quality, with the cream background.

I have redesigned the **Why** page to feature a premium, editorial layout. Each section (Belief, Philosophy, Approach) now pairs the text with a curated, high-quality image that reflects the themes of craftsmanship, spatial depth, and biophilia.

entries.description: Redesigned the Why page with a premium editorial layout, featuring high-quality images for each section (Belief, Philosophy, Approach) and sophisticated typography.

export const INITIAL_BLOGS: BlogPost[] = [ { id: 'biophilic-design', title: 'Biophilic Design: Connecting Architecture with Nature', date: 'October 25, 2023', author: 'Anvitam Team', excerpt: 'Rooted in the belief that humans have an innate connection to nature, biophilic design incorporates natural elements to create spaces that promote well-being.', image: 'https://picsum.photos/1200/600?random=100', toc: [ 'What is biophilic design', 'Principles of Biophilic Design', 'Benefits of Biophilic Design', 'Implementing Biophilic Design', 'Comparison: Conventional vs Biophilic', 'Biophilia in Interiors' ], content: `

What is biophilic design

Biophilic design is an architectural approach that integrates natural elements—such as light, air, greenery, and organic materials—into built environments to promote well-being, productivity, and a deeper connection to nature.

Rooted in the belief that humans have an innate connection to nature, biophilic design incorporates natural elements, materials, and processes to create spaces that promote well-being, enhance productivity, and contribute to environmental sustainability. This approach is not just about aesthetics—it fundamentally improves the quality of life for occupants by fostering a closer connection to the natural world.

Principles of Biophilic Design

Stephen Kellert, a pioneer in biophilic design, identified six core elements that guide its application:

1. Environmental Features

This principle emphasizes the direct integration of natural elements into the built environment. Incorporating plants, water features, sunlight, fresh air, and natural materials such as wood and stone creates spaces that resonate with human affinity for nature.

2. Natural Shapes and Forms

Biophilic architecture employs shapes and patterns found in nature—such as curves, arches, and organic forms—to evoke a sense of harmony.

3. Natural Patterns and Processes

This element focuses on incorporating natural variability, richness of detail, and sensory experiences. Patterns in materials, changing light conditions, and textured surfaces mimic the dynamic processes found in nature.

Benefits of Biophilic Design

  1. Enhances Mental Health: Being surrounded by natural elements has been shown to reduce stress, anxiety, and depression.
  2. Boosts Productivity: Natural lighting and greenery stimulate the mind and reduce fatigue.
  3. Promotes Physical Health: Improved air quality and natural ventilation.

Comparison: Conventional Design vs Biophilic Design

Feature Conventional Design Biophilic Design
Connection to Nature Minimal or incidental 100% Integrated
Materials Used Often synthetic/industrial Natural, Local, Earthy
User Well-being Not always considered Central to design

Biophilia in Interiors

Biophilia shapes not just our architecture but also our interior design philosophy. Across several homes and retreats near Mumbai, we’ve crafted interiors that invite nature inside — through material, light, form, and sensory experience.

We use natural finishes like lime plaster, reclaimed wood, and earth-based flooring to create tactile warmth. Indoor courtyards, skylights, and large openings bring in sunlight, shadows, and breeze, fostering a constant dialogue between inside and out.

Conclusion

In an age of rapid urbanisation, biophilic design offers a return to balance. By weaving nature into the fabric of our homes, we create environments that nurture the human spirit. At Anvitam, we see biophilic design not as a style, but as a responsibility.

` }, { id: 'future-sustainable', title: 'The Future of Sustainable Architecture in Gujarat', date: 'October 15, 2023', author: 'Archana Gavas', excerpt: 'Exploring how traditional Gujarati building techniques can be adapted for modern sustainability needs.', content: '

Full article content regarding sustainable practices in Gujarat...

The biophilic interior isn't just aesthetic; it has palpably shifted the creative energy of our entire team in London.", author: 'Elena Rossi', role: 'Creative Director, Studio Lumière', image: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1438761681033-6461ffad8d80?w=150&h=150&fit=crop&q=80' }, { id: 'tm2', text: "We needed a masterplan that honored the land first. Archana’s permaculture approach wasn't just landscaping; it was a deep ecological restoration. The water harvesting systems are a work of art.", author: 'Julian Thorne', role: 'Founder, Gaia Retreats, Bali', image: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?w=150&h=150&fit=crop&q=80' }, { id: 'tm3', text: "Collaborating with Anvitam on the bamboo-crete prototype was illuminating. Their research-driven design process and willingness to experiment with vernacular materials for modern application is world-class.", author: 'Dr. Klaus Weber', role: 'Lead Researcher, EcoMat Labs, Berlin', image: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1500648767791-00dcc994a43e?w=150&h=150&fit=crop&q=80' }, { id: 'tm4', text: "I didn't want a house; I wanted a gallery for living. Anvitam's play of light and shadow created the perfect canvas for my collection. Sophisticated, understated, and utterly timeless.", author: 'Sarah Al-Fayed', role: 'Art Collector, Dubai', image: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1544005313-94ddf0286df2?w=150&h=150&fit=crop&q=80' } ];
